feedback.text: I fully back the Digital Fairness Act, the upcoming EU proposal set to nail unfair digital practices like dark patterns, addictive design, influencer manipulation, and absurdly tricky subscription traps. It builds on the Digital Services Acts Article 25 and aims to tighten protections across e-commerce, software, gaming-basically anything digital that touches the consumer. But heres the kicker: it doesnt yet address one of the most user-hostile trends out there-shutting down servers that games or devices depend on, turning them into bricks while theyre still being sold. Why this matters: 1. Games like The Crew went offline once Ubisoft pulled the plug. Similarly, Splinter Cell and Assassins Creed 2 now require users to crack the game (removing DRM and always-online checks) just to play what they already own. These titles are still available on Steam, but are in an unplayable state due to broken DRM. Thats NOT fair-whether you bought them yesterday or on launch day. 2. Same deal with hardware or apps tied to servers-if it worked offline at purchase, it should keep working offline. At the very least, users should be allowed to self-host or run a basic fallback. How the DFA can go further: 1. Explicitly guarantee that digital products retain the functionality they had at the time of purchase-even if external services are shut down. 2. Require companies to either maintain offline compatibility or open up the protocols so users can run their own servers. 3. Treat forced feature removal through updates or surprise subscriptions as unfair commercial practices-just like dark patterns or deceptive pricing. I support the DFA wholeheartedly. Its a huge step in fighting shady digital practices-and it should also protect against our purchases turning into paperweights the moment some company flips a switch.
